questions.passage:
Ask someone who has only ever seen a famous painting in books to guess its size, and the guesses go wrong in both directions: the vast canvas imagined as a modest panel, the miniature inflated to fill a wall. The mistake seems trivial — surely the picture is the picture at any size — and that assumption, repeated across a century of reproduction, has quietly deformed our understanding of what paintings are. Size is not packaging. In painting, scale is part of the work's meaning, and to change it is to change the work.

Consider what a very large painting does to you that no reproduction can. It exceeds your visual field. You cannot take it in from where you stand; you must walk, and the walking is part of the composition — the painter has choreographed your approach, your retreat, the moment a form that read as a cliff resolves into brushstrokes. Certain painters of vast color fields insisted their canvases be hung low and viewed from close by, precisely so that the color would surround rather than confront; one spoke of the large picture as intimate, because at that distance you are in it rather than in front of it. A miniature commands the opposite ritual. It draws the viewer into a privacy the size enforces: one person at a time, bent close, at the distance of reading a letter. The devotional panel that fits in two hands and the mural that fills a chapel wall may share a subject; they cannot share a meaning, because they address different bodies doing different things.

Reproduction abolishes all of this, and abolishes it silently. The postcard renders the chapel wall and the pocket panel at the same size, on the same paper, and the lecture slide does the same at a different size — decades of art students learned painting from projections in which every work of every era arrived as a glowing rectangle roughly two meters wide. The dimensions are printed in the caption, of course, and the caption is precisely the problem: it offers as information what the painting delivers as experience. Knowing that a canvas is four meters tall while looking at a hand-sized image of it is like knowing a room is cold while sitting somewhere warm. Scale is felt in the body or it is not grasped at all.

The digital image is usually offered as the cure and is in fact the completed disease. The zoomable photograph lets us glide from the whole composition to the weave of the canvas in a second — a genuinely new way of looking, and a useful one for the study of technique. But it makes every painting all sizes and therefore no size. The work no longer has a scale; it has a zoom level, reset by every viewer, anchored to nothing. A generation is now learning pictures this way, and what it is learning, beneath the pleasure, is that a painting is an image — weightless, dimensionless, indifferent to the body — when a painting is in fact an object that was made at one size, for bodies, on purpose.

The objection writes itself: we cannot all travel, and reproduction has democratized what would otherwise belong to the few who can. True, and worth the price — as a map is worth having when the country is far away. No one, however, calls the map the country. The danger is not reproduction but forgetting, the slow slide by which the substitute becomes the standard and the original comes to seem a mere enlargement of its own postcard. Stand finally before the painting you have known for twenty years from books, feel it tower or shrink, and the shock you feel is the work correcting you. That shock is not a bonus for the lucky traveler. It is the painting, arriving at last at its own size.
questions.stem: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Digital zoom tools should be removed entirely from museum websites and online collection databases because they systematically teach a generation of viewers to ignore overall composition in favor of minute details of technique.
choices.B: Reproductions of paintings have democratized access to art at no significant cost to how paintings are understood.
choices.C: Because scale is constitutive of a painting's meaning, reproduction that alters or dissolves size changes the work, and treating images as substitutes for objects distorts our understanding of painting.
choices.D: Large paintings are inherently more meaningful than small ones because they engage the viewer's entire body in motion.

explanations.A: The passage calls zooming 'a genuinely new way of looking, and a useful one for the study of technique'; the criticism is of what the format teaches about paintings' nature, and no removal of tools is proposed.
explanations.B: The passage concedes democratization is 'worth the price' but insists there is a price — the slide by which 'the substitute becomes the standard'; reading the concession as cost-free deletes half the argument.
explanations.C: Correct. The thesis is stated early — 'scale is part of the work's meaning, and to change it is to change the work' — developed through the large canvas, the miniature, the postcard, and the zoom, and completed by the map analogy: reproduction is valuable so long as no one 'calls the map the country.'
explanations.D: The passage assigns the miniature its own equally meaningful ritual — the enforced privacy, the distance of reading a letter — and argues the two sizes 'cannot share a meaning,' not that one size ranks above the other.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, the claim that the zoomable image makes every painting 'all sizes and therefore no size' most nearly means that:

choices.A: digital photographs of paintings are typically captured at resolutions exceeding what any viewer requires.
choices.B: when a work's apparent size is reset by each viewer at will, the fixed scale the painter built into the object drops out of the encounter.
choices.C: zoomable images display paintings at their true dimensions only when viewers consult the accompanying captions.
choices.D: digital viewing makes small paintings appear considerably more impressive than large ones by allowing effectively unlimited magnification of their painted surfaces.

explanations.A: Resolution measures the density of the file's detail, not the work's presented size; the passage's complaint concerns the loss of a fixed scale, which no resolution restores.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage glosses the phrase in the next sentence: 'The work no longer has a scale; it has a zoom level, reset by every viewer, anchored to nothing.' The painting was 'made at one size, for bodies, on purpose,' and infinite adjustability removes exactly that fixed fact.
explanations.C: The passage has already dispatched captions in the previous paragraph — they offer 'as information what the painting delivers as experience' — so consulting them cannot supply what the phrase says is lost.
explanations.D: No comparison of small and large paintings' impressiveness under magnification appears; the point is that all works alike lose their anchoring size, not that some gain advantage.

questions.stem: The author mentions painters of vast color fields who insisted their canvases be hung low and viewed from close by primarily in order to:

choices.A: demonstrate that artists' detailed installation instructions are frequently disregarded by the very museums and galleries that display their work.
choices.B: concede that some large paintings were designed to be experienced as if they were reproductions.
choices.C: argue that abstract painting depends on scale more heavily than figurative painting does.
choices.D: show that painters have deliberately composed with the viewer's bodily position, treating scale and distance as part of the work.

explanations.A: No museum's compliance or defiance is discussed; the instructions appear as evidence of the painters' intentions, not as the occasion for a complaint about institutions.
explanations.B: The instruction aims at surrounding the viewer with color so that 'you are in it rather than in front of it' — an effect the passage argues no reproduction can produce, making this reading a reversal.
explanations.C: The passage draws no contrast between abstract and figurative work; the same paragraph moves directly to devotional panels and chapel murals, applying the point across kinds of painting.
explanations.D: Correct. The example supports the paragraph's claim that 'the painter has choreographed your approach': hanging height and viewing distance were specified so the color would 'surround rather than confront,' showing scale and the viewer's body treated as compositional material.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings, if true, would most weaken the author's central argument?

choices.A: Studies find that viewers' interpretations and felt responses to paintings are essentially unchanged whether they encounter the original at full size or a hand-sized reproduction of it.
choices.B: Surveys show that most museum visitors spend less than thirty seconds in front of each painting they view.
choices.C: Research shows that art students who study from digital images can recall the printed dimensions of major works with high accuracy.
choices.D: Historians of art education establish that projection slides of uniform width were originally adopted across universities for mundane reasons of equipment cost rather than for any considered pedagogical theory.

explanations.A: Correct. The argument rests on the claim that scale 'is felt in the body or it is not grasped at all,' so that changing size changes the work as experienced. If responses and interpretations survive the change of size intact, size is packaging after all, and the central thesis fails.
explanations.B: Brief viewing times say nothing about whether size shapes what is experienced during the encounter; a thirty-second confrontation with a towering canvas remains bodily in the way the passage describes.
explanations.C: Accurate recall of printed dimensions is exactly what the passage dismisses as caption knowledge — information rather than experience, like knowing a room is cold while sitting somewhere warm — so it confirms rather than threatens the distinction.
explanations.D: The origin of the uniform slide is incidental; the argument concerns what uniform presentation does to understanding, whatever motive introduced the equipment.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the zoomable digital image is best described as:

choices.A: wholly hostile, denying that the format offers anything of value to any viewer.
choices.B: enthusiastic, welcoming it as the first reproduction technology genuinely able to preserve what matters in painting.
choices.C: sharply critical of its deeper lesson, while granting its genuine usefulness for the study of technique.
choices.D: indifferent, treating it as merely the latest in a series of equivalent reproduction technologies.

explanations.A: The passage explicitly credits zooming as 'a genuinely new way of looking, and a useful one for the study of technique,' a concession that total hostility would not make.
explanations.B: The author calls the digital image 'the completed disease,' the format that finishes what the postcard began; welcoming it as preservation inverts the verdict.
explanations.C: Correct. The judgment is double: the tool is useful for technique, yet what a generation 'is learning, beneath the pleasure, is that a painting is an image — weightless, dimensionless, indifferent to the body.' The attack falls on the format's implicit teaching, not on its every use.
explanations.D: Far from equivalent, the zoomable image is singled out as worse than its predecessors — the postcard fixed a false size, while the zoom dissolves size altogether — so indifference misstates the escalation the passage describes.

questions.stem: The author's analogy of the map and the country suggests that reproductions of paintings are best regarded as:

choices.A: unavoidable distortions whose cumulative harms outweigh their benefits for everyone except professional scholars.
choices.B: valuable aids for those at a distance from the original, dangerous only when mistaken for the thing itself.
choices.C: temporary conveniences that improving technology will eventually render obsolete.
choices.D: neutral records whose accuracy depends entirely on the quality of the photography.

explanations.A: The author affirms that reproduction's democratization is 'worth the price' — a general endorsement, not one restricted to scholars — and locates the danger elsewhere, in forgetting.
explanations.B: Correct. A map is 'worth having when the country is far away,' but 'no one calls the map the country': the reproduction earns its keep as a substitute at a distance, and the harm named is the 'slow slide by which the substitute becomes the standard.'
explanations.C: The passage predicts no obsolescence; its worry runs the opposite way — that the newest formats deepen rather than retire the substitution, as the zoomable image shows.
explanations.D: Photographic quality is never the issue; even a perfect image renders the chapel wall and the pocket panel alike, because what is lost — scale addressed to the body — is not a matter of fidelity.
